metadata.topic: Adaptation, training, and scaling choices relevant to agent systems
metadata.research_question: How do papers relevant to agent-oriented AI systems intervene at different levels of the stack—model adaptation, policy learning from interaction, and system-level scaling—and what does each level make visible or leave unresolved?

topic_scope.topic: Adaptation, training, and scaling choices relevant to agent systems
topic_scope.research_question: How do papers relevant to agent-oriented AI systems intervene at different levels of the stack—model adaptation, policy learning from interaction, and system-level scaling—and what does each level make visible or leave unresolved?
topic_scope.scope_summary: This review uses a heterogeneous three-paper seed set to compare distinct intervention layers relevant to agent-oriented AI systems rather than to rank directly benchmark-comparable methods.
inclusion_criteria: Include seed papers that represent distinct intervention layers relevant to agent-oriented AI systems.
Keep papers that help compare model adaptation, agent learning from interaction, and system-level scaling/evaluation.
exclusion_criteria: Exclude claims of direct empirical superiority across the entire seed set.
Exclude benchmark-style winner/loser conclusions unless the papers are directly comparable on the same task.
known_scope_risks: The seed set is heterogeneous and not a same-task benchmark family.
LoRA is relevant as a model-adaptation anchor but is not natively a direct agent-systems paper in the same sense as the other two.
seed_paper_rationale: OpenClaw-RL anchors the agent training and interaction-learning layer.
Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems anchors the system-scaling and evaluation layer.

papers.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
papers.inclusion_reason: Included as a model-adaptation anchor relevant to how agent-oriented systems can change behavior efficiently.
papers.comparison_readiness_note: Ready for grounded intervention-level and claim-aware comparison as a Layer 1-complete model-adaptation anchor.

papers.title: OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
papers.inclusion_reason: Included as an agent-training anchor centered on next-state signals and RL for personal and general agents.
papers.comparison_readiness_note: Ready for grounded comparison on training-layer claims, evidence style, and online learning assumptions.

papers.title: Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
papers.inclusion_reason: Included as a system-level scaling and evaluation anchor for agent architectures and coordination trade-offs.
papers.comparison_readiness_note: Ready for grounded comparison on system-level evidence, scaling principles, and coordination trade-offs.

coverage_summary: This example is now backed by full Layer 1 artifact sets for all three papers.

cells.summary: Model-adaptation layer via low-rank parameter updates on frozen pretrained weights.
Agent-training layer via next-state-driven RL and on-policy distillation from live interaction streams.
System-scaling and evaluation layer via controlled comparison of single-agent and multi-agent coordination topologies.
Adapt large models efficiently while preserving downstream task quality and minimizing trainable parameters, memory cost, and latency overhead.
Turn next-state feedback into a live online learning source for personal and general agents.
Derive predictive rules for when different agent-system architectures help or hurt on agentic tasks.
Method proposal backed by benchmark tables on adaptation quality, parameter count, latency, and large-model efficiency observations.
Agent-training framework backed by personal-agent and general-agent experiments, plus algorithm and prompt appendices.
Controlled architecture study backed by multi-benchmark experiments, coordination metrics, and a predictive mixed-effects model.
Indirectly relevant to agent systems through the base-model adaptation layer rather than through end-to-end agent behavior.
Directly targets personal agents and general-purpose agents across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings.
Directly compares single-agent and multi-agent systems across several coordination architectures and task domains.
Does not address online interaction learning or multi-agent coordination, so its relevance to agent systems is important but indirect.
Framework generality is promising but broader universality claims still outrun the currently reported settings and judge stack.
Offers strong architecture-selection evidence, but the resulting principles remain bounded by the benchmark suite, architecture set, and metric design.

coverage_warnings: The set is heterogeneous and still should not be used for direct winner/loser benchmarking across the three papers.
All three papers now have full Layer 1 artifacts, so comparison can move from seed-level topic mapping to grounded layer-aware synthesis.
comparison_limits: Cross-paper comparison is strongest at the level of intervention layers, evidence styles, and unresolved questions rather than same-task superiority.
LoRA remains indirectly relevant to agent systems compared with the other two papers, so symmetry of scope should not be overstated.

taxonomy.name: Model adaptation
taxonomy.grouping_rationale: Changes the underlying model efficiently before or alongside downstream use.

taxonomy.name: Agent learning from interaction
taxonomy.grouping_rationale: Improves deployed agents online using next-state signals from interaction traces.

taxonomy.name: System scaling and coordination evaluation
taxonomy.grouping_rationale: Studies when different coordination structures improve or degrade agent-system performance.

evidence_patterns.pattern: Each paper supports a different layer of the stack with a different evidence style: adaptation benchmarking, online learning experiments, and controlled architecture evaluation.
evidence_patterns.coverage_note: full_layer1 coverage across all three papers

evidence_patterns.pattern: The strongest cross-paper synthesis is not about a single best method, but about what becomes visible when intervention shifts from model weights to live interaction learning to coordination structure.

gaps_and_disagreements.issue: The three papers expose complementary but non-substitutable layers of improvement: efficient adaptation, online agent learning, and system-level coordination science.
gaps_and_disagreements.why_it_matters: A team choosing only one layer may miss bottlenecks that appear at another layer of the stack.

gaps_and_disagreements.issue: LoRA offers strong practical efficiency for model adaptation, but it does not answer whether deployed agents learn from interaction or whether multi-agent coordination is worthwhile.
gaps_and_disagreements.why_it_matters: This prevents overgeneralizing model-layer gains into agent- or system-level conclusions.

gaps_and_disagreements.issue: OpenClaw-RL argues that next-state signals can train many agents online, while the scaling paper shows that adding coordination structure often creates overhead and can degrade performance on some tasks.
gaps_and_disagreements.why_it_matters: Online training and multi-agent scaling are not the same intervention; a stronger training loop does not automatically justify more coordinated agents.

recommended_reading_order.order_rationale: Start at the model layer to establish what efficient adaptation can and cannot solve.
Then move to the agent-training layer to see how deployed systems can improve from interaction signals beyond offline adaptation.
Finish at the system layer to evaluate when coordination structure itself helps or hurts after model and training choices are fixed.

review_limits: This remains a heterogeneous three-paper set, not a same-task benchmark family.
LoRA is included as a model-adaptation anchor relevant to agent systems, not as a direct online-agent or coordination paper.
The synthesis is now grounded by full Layer 1 artifacts for all three papers, but cross-paper superiority claims still remain structurally limited.
next_actions: Add one or two neighboring papers around each layer if you want denser within-layer comparison.
Use the current three-paper set as a worked example of stack-level intervention analysis rather than leaderboard comparison.
Extend the review with a paper that explicitly links adaptation and online agent learning if you want a bridge between LoRA and OpenClaw-RL.
